content:
The biggest mistake wasn’t using AI. It was forgetting that I was still the developer.

3 min read
Just now

--

Opening
A few months ago, I caught myself doing something I never thought I would.
I copied an AI-generated solution into my project without fully understanding why it worked.
At the time, it felt efficient.
After all, the code looked clean.
The explanation sounded convincing.
And AI had helped me solve similar problems before.
So I trusted it.
More than I trusted myself.
That was my biggest mistake.

When Confidence Becomes a Shortcut
AI is incredibly persuasive.
It rarely says,
“I’m not sure.”
Instead, it presents answers with remarkable confidence.
As developers, it’s surprisingly easy to mistake confidence for correctness.
I did exactly that.
Whenever I got stuck, my first instinct stopped being:
“Let me think about this.”
It became:
“Let me ask AI.”
Slowly, I stopped solving problems.
I started outsourcing my thinking.
I didn’t notice it happening.
The Bug That Wasn’t AI’s Fault
One day, AI suggested a solution for a problem I was facing in my React Native project.
It looked reasonable.
The syntax was correct.
Everything compiled.
But something still felt wrong.
After spending more time debugging, I realized the issue wasn’t the code AI had written.
The issue was that I had asked the wrong question.
AI had solved exactly what I asked.
It just wasn’t the problem I actually had.
That realization changed everything.

AI Doesn’t Know Your Project
One lesson became painfully clear.
AI doesn’t understand your product.
It doesn’t know your architecture.
It doesn’t know your users.
It doesn’t know the technical debt hiding inside your codebase.
It only knows what you tell it.
And sometimes, what you tell it isn’t enough.
That’s not a weakness of AI.
It’s a reminder of my responsibility as the developer.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The Better Way to Use AI
Since then, my workflow has completely changed.
Instead of asking AI to solve problems for me, I ask it to help me think.
I ask questions like:

• Why might this approach fail?

• What are the trade-offs?

• Can you explain this concept differently?

• What assumptions am I making?
The quality of my questions improved.
And so did the quality of the answers.
Ironically, I started getting more value from AI the moment I relied on it less.

Final Thoughts
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Building a real product taught me that good software comes from good decisions — not just good code.Today, I use AI more than ever.
But I trust myself more than ever, too.
Not because I’m smarter than AI.
Because I understand something I didn’t before.
AI isn’t responsible for the quality of my software.
I am.
The biggest mistake wasn’t trusting AI.
The biggest mistake was believing that confidence was the same as correctness.
The day I stopped treating AI as the final answer was the day it became the most valuable tool in my workflow.
